problem_summary: 2017 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research): model the airport security checkpoint process to explain why O'Hare-style checkpoints suffer long, high-variance passenger wait times, then propose and quantitatively evaluate modifications that reduce both the mean and variance of total throughput time, including the effect of TSA Pre-Check, a special-population (elderly/children) policy, and a transfer to Chinese-airport conditions.
models_used: Discrete-event queueing simulation (custom C++ engine) as the backbone — passengers flow through Zone A ID-check queues, Zone B screening lanes, a prepare/screen station, and a Zone D additional-inspection queue, with FIFO queues and shortest-queue join
Inflow model: passenger inter-arrival times generated as a Poisson process (separate arrival seeds for Pre-Check/VIP vs regular passengers), implemented via an inverse-transform poisson() sampler

Attributes-generation model: per-passenger random attributes — ID-check time ~ Gaussian(11.31s, 3.70s), millimeter-wave body scan ~ Gaussian(11.64s, 5.82s), luggage count ~ uniform{0..3}, prepare time (45±10s regular / 15±5s Pre-Check, plus 5s per bag), and an error flag (0.01 VIP / 0.03 regular) routing to Zone D ~ Gaussian(120s,20s); Gaussian draws via Box-Muller (gaussrand)
Screening model: screening time = max(t_milli, t_xray) to capture parallel body-scan and baggage X-ray as the binding constraint

MPL (Multi-Passenger Linkage) model: a pipelined prepare station holding N_prepare passengers simultaneously (modeled as a bounded buffer / linkage queue) so a new passenger starts preparing as soon as one leaves; optimum found at N_prepare=3

PBQ (Priority-Based Queuing) model: lanes split into 1 Pre-Check / 2 regular / 1 emergency; passengers tagged by t_ad (time-to-departure) into priority / regular / over-punctual groups with thresholds t_ad1, t_ad2 controlling emergency-lane access — a time-window priority discipline
SP (Special Population) model: elderly/children modeled as Pre-Check-equivalent passengers carrying no luggage (screening time = body-scan time only)
Cut-in-line model: China-transfer variant adding per-passenger cut probability R_cut and a uniformly random insertion position to test robustness
Logistic / 'Natural Growth Model' curve fit y = A/(1+exp(-B(x-C)^D))+E fitted to T_avg vs Pre-Check ratio, R^2 = 0.99, to characterize the S-shaped saturation
key_techniques: Calibrating all distribution parameters (means/SDs) directly from the supplied COMAP Excel data via a finite-difference preprocessing pass rather than inventing numbers — a separate C++ utility computes per-process mean/variance from raw timestamps

Decomposing total time T into its six constituent steps and plotting the percentage breakdown — the single most decisive analytical move (waiting-for-screening = 98.11% of T), which localizes the bottleneck and justifies every downstream modification
Using variance/standard deviation of T as a first-class objective alongside the mean, directly answering the problem's 'high variance' complaint instead of only minimizing average wait
Parameter sweeps to optimize design knobs: N_prepare (2-6), R_lane (1:3, 2:2, 3:1), R_passenger (0-10), and a 2D (t_ad1, t_ad2) grid producing heatmaps to locate optima
Before/after pie charts showing the bottleneck share dropping from 98.11% to 57.21% under MPL — a vivid, self-evident evidence of impact
Robustness/sensitivity framed as a real-world transfer (US -> China with cut-in-line behavior) rather than abstract perturbation, showing both models retain effectiveness
Curve-fitting an OR result to a known biological growth law to give an interpretable closed form and saturation argument
why_it_won: "It nailed the judge-pleasing arc: a clean, data-calibrated simulation that first DIAGNOSES the problem with a single killer statistic (waiting-for-screening = 98.11% of all time), then proposes targeted, cheap, physically realistic interventions (MPL pipelined prep zone, PBQ time-window priority lane) and proves their effect quantitatively on BOTH mean and variance — exactly the two things the prompt asked to reduce. The modifications are operationally actionable (e.g., 'add a small zone allowing 3 passengers to prepare at once'), not hand-wavy. It went beyond the ask with two thoughtful extensions (evaluating TSA's real elderly/children policy and honestly concluding it barely helps; transferring the model to Chinese airports with cutting-in-line), demonstrating maturity and self-criticism. The fault-tolerance argument for PBQ (works even when t_ad1/t_ad2 are mis-set) shows engineering judgment. Full reproducible C++ source is appended."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet leads with the conclusion and names the two deliverable models (MPL, PBQ) plus headline findings — judge can grasp the whole contribution in 30 seconds. Structure follows textbook OR flow: Restatement -> Assumptions+Notations -> Basic (diagnostic) Model -> Modifications -> Evaluation/Sensitivity -> Conclusion with explicit Strengths/Weaknesses and a manager-facing recommendations list. The proportion-of-time pie chart (Fig 3) and the before/after MPL pie pair (Figs 10-11) are the rhetorical spine — they make the bottleneck and the fix visually undeniable. Assumptions are each paired with a justification. A dedicated 'Suggestions to the Security Manager' section translates math into plain operational advice."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The transcribed figure data tables are clearly garbled/inconsistent (e.g., Fig 8 NoPrepare values, Fig 13 axis labels, Fig 24 nonsensical t_a values) — chart quality/readability in the original may have been weaker than the prose claims, and several numbers don't cross-check

Heavy reliance on Poisson arrivals with a single rate ignores time-of-day non-stationarity (real rush-hour surges are non-homogeneous); the model fakes 'peak flow' only by shrinking the arrival interval t_a rather than a true time-varying intensity
Modeling elderly/children simply as luggage-free Pre-Check passengers is acknowledged as crude and likely drove the (possibly understated) 'policy barely helps' conclusion
Cut-in-line probability assumed constant and independent of congestion — the authors themselves flag this as unrealistic
No statistical confidence intervals or replication-count reporting on the simulation outputs; single-run-style curves with no error bars, so 'significant' reductions aren't formally tested
Emergency/incident scenarios explicitly excluded, so conclusions hold only in 'peaceful' steady operation
The logistic 'Natural Growth Model' fit is an analogy with no mechanistic basis for the specific functional form beyond a good R^2
Appendix C++ code is partially corrupted in transcription (mismatched braces, 'posson'/'input' typos), reducing true reproducibility
reusable_patterns: Diagnose-then-fix structure: build a faithful baseline simulation first, compute a time/cost breakdown to find the dominant bottleneck, and let that single statistic justify every proposed modification

Calibrate-from-provided-data discipline: write a small preprocessing script to extract means/SDs from the contest's raw data file instead of guessing parameters, and state each in a notation/assumptions table
Optimize on mean AND variance whenever the prompt mentions unpredictability or congestion — variance is often the real deliverable
Parameter-sweep + heatmap/curve to locate optima for each design knob, then report the optimal value plainly (N_prepare=3, R_lane=1:3)
Before/after pie or bar charts of the bottleneck's share to make impact visually self-evident
Honesty extension: test the real-world official policy in the problem and report even when it underperforms — judges reward this over cheerleading
Robustness-as-transfer: re-run the model in a genuinely different real context (different country/behavior) as the sensitivity analysis
Fault-tolerance argument: show your policy still helps when its tuning parameters are mis-set, proving operational practicality
End with a plain-language 'recommendations to the decision-maker' section that converts every model result into one actionable bullet

Append runnable simulation code and a discrete-event engine template (Poisson inflow via inverse-transform, Gaussian via Box-Muller, FIFO queues + shortest-queue join, screening = max of parallel servers)
